scenario: Nimbus Bank releases a Camunda 8.8 invoice approval process that handles 6000 invoices per day. The team has fast unit tests running against an isolated workflow engine for every commit, and the release manager must define what additional verification is required before each production deployment of the process.
question: Which testing approach does the Camunda 8.8 guide recommend for verifying a BPMN process implementation before each release?

options.text: Run only the fast unit tests against the isolated workflow engine and skip integration tests, treating the unit coverage as sufficient for any release candidate
Run integration tests in an environment that closely mirrors the production setup, possibly including human-driven exploratory integration testing of the process
Run only manual smoke tests directly on the production cluster and skip automated integration tests, treating production latencies as the only real signal of readiness
Run a fresh suite of unit tests against a brand-new throwaway in-memory engine for every release, replacing the existing fast unit test suite each time

a.text: Incorrect. The guide says you should also verify your implementation with integration tests in a production-like environment before releasing, even when fast unit tests already cover the process model in isolation.

b.text: Correct. The guide says to verify your implementation with integration tests in an environment that closely mirrors the production setup, which may include human-driven exploratory integration testing of the process.

c.text: Incorrect. The guide pairs fast automated unit tests with integration tests in a production-like environment; running only manual smoke tests in production is not the recommended verification strategy before release.

d.text: Incorrect. Throwing away the existing unit test suite is not part of the guidance; the recommendation is to combine fast unit tests with integration tests in a production-like environment before each release.

explanation: Camunda recommends testing BPMN processes like any other software: fast automated unit tests against a localized isolated engine, plus integration tests in an environment that mirrors production before each release.
